term: Name the 12 Steps of Hatha Yoga Surya Namaskar (Bihar School)
definition:
1. Exhale, hands at heart in prayer position — Pranamasana
2. Inhale, raise your arms — Hasta Uttanasana
3. Exhale, touch your toes — Padahastasana
4. Inhale, step left leg back — Ashwa Sanchalanasana
5. Exhale, step right leg back, raise your hips — Parvatasana
6. In the same breath, knees, chest, chin to the ground — Ashtanga Namaskara
7. Inhale, raise your chest — Bhujangasana
8. Exhale, back to the mountain — Parvatasana
9. Inhale, step the left leg forward — Ashwa Sanchalanasana
10. Exhale, step the right leg forward — Padahastasana
11. Inhale, raise your arms — Hasta Uttanasana
12. Exhale, hands to prayer position — Pranamasana
13. (repeat with opposite legs)

term: 3–4 Prep Exercises for Downward Dog — and why
definition:
1. Cat-Cow: warms the spine, mobilizes wrists and shoulders
2. Plank Pose: builds shoulder stability and core strength needed to hold DD
3. Puppy Pose (Uttana Shishosana): opens upper back and stretches lats, mimicking DD arm extension
4. Standing Forward Fold (Uttanasana): lengthens hamstrings and calves, easing posterior chain tension that prevents heels from grounding

1st limb — ethical restraints toward others:
Ahimsa (non-violence)
Satya (truthfulness)
Asteya (non-stealing)
Brahmacharya (continence)
Aparigraha (non-possessiveness)

2nd limb — personal observances and self-disciplines:
Saucha (cleanliness)
Santosha (contentment)
Tapas (austerity)
Svadhyaya (self-study)
Ishvara Pranidhana (surrender to the divine)

3rd limb — sthiran sukham asanam: a stable (physically and emotionally) and comfortable posture where alignment is correct; also a requirement of pranayama: a stable, comfortable posture that allows free breath movement and sustained concentration
4th limb — breath control; regulation and extension of prana (life force) through breathing techniques
5th limb — withdrawal of the senses; drawing awareness inward and away from external stimuli
6th limb — concentration; sustained, focused attention on a single point or object

7th limb — meditation; unbroken, continuous flow of concentration leading toward absorption; also a requirement of pranayama: directing focused attention to a specific chakra or internal focal point during practice
8th limb — blissful absorption; complete union with the object of meditation; enlightenment
Drishti: gaze fixed at the tip of the nose

Decreasing the angle between two body parts by bending a joint (e.g., bending the knee or elbow)
Increasing the angle between two body parts by straightening a joint (e.g., straightening the knee or elbow)
Movement of a limb toward the midline of the body
Movement of a limb away from the midline of the body
Rotation of the forearm so the palm faces downward; also refers to inward rolling of the foot/ankle
Rotation of the forearm so the palm faces upward; also refers to outward rolling of the foot/ankle
Movement of a body part around its own longitudinal axis
Flexion of the foot upward toward the shin, decreasing the angle at the ankle joint
Extension of the foot downward away from the shin (pointing the toes), increasing the angle at the ankle joint
Antaraya: physical disease or ailment (e.g., chronic pain) that disrupts practice
Antaraya: mental dullness or lack of interest; apathy toward practice
Antaraya: doubt; uncertainty about the path or one's own capability
Antaraya: carelessness or procrastination; neglecting consistent practice
Antaraya: physical laziness; heaviness of body that resists effort
Antaraya: craving of the senses; inability to detach from sensory pleasures
Antaraya: false perception or misconception; deluded understanding of the practice or self
Antaraya: failure to attain the ground of higher practice; unable to reach advanced stages
Antaraya: inability to maintain or stabilize attainments; falling back from achieved states
Breath retention; holding the breath after inhalation (antara kumbhaka) or after exhalation (bahya kumbhaka)
Internal breath retention; holding the breath after a full inhalation; builds prana and heat, stimulates the nervous system
External breath retention; holding the breath after a full exhalation; creates stillness, draws energy inward, and strengthens the bandhas
Gestures or "mental attitudes" that redirect prana; 108 mudras are available
Energy locks that contain and direct prana (e.g., Mula Bandha, Uddiyana Bandha, Jalandhara Bandha)
Mental chant used to tame and focus the mind during pranayama practice
Path of knowledge and wisdom; liberation through study, self-inquiry, and discernment between the real and unreal
Path of devotion; liberation through love, surrender, and worship of the divine
Path of selfless action; liberation through service performed without attachment to results
The royal path; liberation through meditation and the 8 limbs of Patanjali's Ashtanga system
Astika: orthodox school of logic and epistemology; establishes the four valid means of knowledge (Pramanas)
Astika: orthodox school of atomism; categorizes all reality into substances, qualities, and actions
Astika: orthodox dualistic school; enumerates 25 tattvas arising from Purusha (pure consciousness) and Prakriti (matter)
Astika: orthodox school of Patanjali; practical 8-limbed path to liberate Purusha from Prakriti through discipline and meditation
Astika: orthodox school of Vedic ritual interpretation; emphasizes correct performance of dharma and sacrificial rites
Astika: orthodox school exploring the nature of Brahman; encompasses Advaita (non-dualism), Dvaita (dualism), and Vishishtadvaita among others
Nastika: heterodox school founded by Siddhartha Gautama; rejects Vedic authority; teaches the Four Noble Truths, Eightfold Path, impermanence, and no-self (anatta)
Nastika: heterodox school associated with Mahavira; rejects Vedic authority; teaches ahimsa, anekantavada (non-absolutism), and liberation through non-attachment
Nastika: heterodox materialist school; rejects the Vedas and all metaphysics; holds that only direct perception (pratyaksha) is valid knowledge
Dosha composed of air (Vayu) and space (Akasha); governs movement, creativity, and the nervous system; imbalance manifests as anxiety, dryness, and irregular digestion
Dosha composed of fire (Agni) and water (Jala); governs transformation, digestion, and metabolism; imbalance manifests as anger, inflammation, and overheating
Dosha composed of earth (Prithvi) and water (Jala); governs stability, lubrication, and structure; imbalance manifests as lethargy, congestion, and weight gain
Yama: non-violence; refraining from harm in thought, word, and deed toward all living beings
Yama: truthfulness; alignment of thought, speech, and action with truth
Yama: non-stealing; not taking what has not been freely given, including time, ideas, or energy
Yama: right use of energy or continence; traditionally celibacy; broadly the conservation and redirection of vital energy away from sensory indulgence
Yama: non-possessiveness; not hoarding; taking only what is necessary and releasing attachment to outcomes
Shatkarma: internal cleansing; 13 types including voluntary vomiting (Kunjal Kriya) and cloth swallowing (Vastra Dhauti); purifies the digestive tract
Shatkarma: yogic enema; cleansing of the colon using water; removes waste and balances Apana Vayu
Shatkarma: nasal passage cleansing; Jala Neti uses warm saline water; Sutra Neti uses a waxed thread; clears sinuses and supports pranayama
Shatkarma: abdominal massage through isolation and churning of the rectus abdominis; massages internal organs and stimulates digestive fire (Agni)
Shatkarma: one-pointed gazing, typically at a candle flame without blinking; purifies and strengthens the eyes; develops concentration (Dharana)
Shatkarma: skull-shining breath; forceful rapid exhalations with passive inhalations; cleanses the respiratory tract and energizes the mind
Antahkarana: the indecisive mind; receives and processes sensory input; generates doubts and mental fluctuations (vrittis)
Antahkarana: the intellect; the decisive faculty; discerns, determines, and provides clarity and wisdom
Antahkarana: memory and the subconscious; the storehouse of impressions (samskaras) and past experiences
Antahkarana: the ego; the "I"-maker; creates the sense of individual identity, ownership, and doership
A connected series of asanas with synchronized breath that functions as moving meditation; benefits include improved focus and reduced anxiety, depression, stress responses, and insomnia
